title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Trading Volumes and Values Dip, While Prices Experience Marginal Recovery
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(17th March â€“ 23rdÂ March)
  • A total of 202,162 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 31.2% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades also witnessed a fall of 58.4% WoW as 629,662 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price recovered marginally this week to $59.94/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 4.43% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 1.58% in price, reaching $60.97/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 5.98% WoW; on 27th March, the spot price stood at $59.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $63.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.81% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 2.81% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Over the past week, trading in CA LCFS credits has seen a significant drop, with both Type 1 and non-zero credit volumes falling. While the prices for Type 1 and non-zero trades have increased marginally, the overall trading values in both categories have taken a substantial hit. The timeline for the amended CA LCFS program remains unclear as CARB gets ready to present an updated version of the rules for public comment after the OAL disapproved them in February. Meanwhile, biofuel and traditional fuel groups nationwide are advocating for the repeal of Californiaâ€™s EV mandate, which, if successful, could color the trajectory of the LCFS program. This uncertainty has led to reduced activity and a bearish outlook within the LCFS program. Additionally, the EPA has introduced several deregulatory measures that could threaten Californiaâ€™s EV advancements. CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend [caption id="attachment_594442" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.43% WoW rise, increasing from $57.40 to $59.94 this week, while all non-zero credit prices increased by 1.58% WoW, from $60.02 to $60.97. Type 1 trades saw 202,162 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 31.20% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a decline of 58.40% to reach 629,662 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_594405" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 5.98% WoW, reaching $59.75/MT. On 27th March, the average spot price was $59.00/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $63.50/MT.  

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_594406" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 54 trades were reported in the market, with 3 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $77.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 10,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.5 per credit of Type 2, reported for transactions at volumes of 5,000 and 10,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 502 credits of the Type 2 category, undertaken for a price of $72. The largest volume transfers of 85,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $69.25.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_594438" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 spot price in February stood at $72.15, with total trading volumes reaching 6.31 MMT. By March 23, the Type 1 average spot price had dropped to $58.20, while trading volumes reached 3.24 MMT. In the absence of an unanticipated uptick in trading activity in the last of week the month, March would be the third consecutive month when trading volumes have declined month-over-month. By the end of March last year, the average Type 1 spot price stood at $64.93 and volumes traded stood at 3.98 MMT.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_594440" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 17th March â€“ 23rd March, total VPT recorded a 4.50% increase(WoW), marking the first rise in VPT in the past five weeks.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.81% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 2.81% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Growth Energy to USDA: Maximize Flexibility on CSA Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, presented recommendations on how to improve and implement the U.S. Department of Agricultureâ€™s (USDA) interim guidelines on climate-smart, or regenerative, agriculture practices today. The agency is currently reviewing the Interim Rule on Technical Guidelines for Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) Crops Used as Biofuel Feedstocks.
  • Clean Fuels, Farm, Feedstock Groups Ask EPA to Set 5.25-Billion-Gallon 2026 RFS BBD Volume Clean Fuels Alliance America, American Farm Bureau Federation, American Soybean Association, National Oilseed Processors Association, North American Renderers Association, and U.S. Canola Association sent a letter to EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin encouraging the agency to establish robust, timely Renewable Fuel Standard volumes for 2026 and beyond. The letter asks that EPA propose and finalize the 2026 biomass-based diesel volume at 5.25 billion gallons.
  • SoCalGas Announces First Renewable Natural Gas Contract Approved Under California Program Southern California Gas Company (SoCalGas) today announced it executed a contract with Organic Energy Solutions (OES) to procure renewable natural gas (RNG) converted from organic waste and inject it into SoCalGasâ€™ pipeline system. The contract is the first approved by the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) under Senate Bill (SB) 1440 which sets specific RNG procurement targets for the stateâ€™s natural gas utilities. The RNG will be sourced from a project located in the city of San Bernardino and is an important step toward achieving Californiaâ€™s goal to reduce methane emissions from agriculture and waste while advancing energy decarbonization in the state.
  • Washington House Passes Bill to Strengthen Clean Fuel Standard, Boost Emission Cuts The Washington State House of Representatives has passed HB 1409, a bill aimed at strengthening the state's Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) to further cut transportation emissions and boost clean energy investments. The updated CFS will drive greater pollution reductions, expand EV charging infrastructure, and promote local job creation while encouraging the use of sustainable biofuels. With transportation accounting for 40% of the stateâ€™s emissions, the CFS plays a key role in Washingtonâ€™s climate strategy, aligning with similar programs in California, Oregon, and British Columbia. By 2034, the strengthened policy could potentially double emission reductions, making cleaner fuels more accessible and cost-effective for Washington residents.
  • 100+ Energy, Ag & Transportation Groups Urge Congress to Reverse CA Vehicle Ban The American Fuel & Petrochemical Manufacturers (AFPM) joined 110 other stakeholders across energy, agricultural and transportation supply chains urging Congressional leadership to use the Congressional Review Act (CRA) to protect American consumers and reverse Californiaâ€™s harmful vehicle mandates, including its expansive ban on sales of new gas, diesel and traditional hybrid cars and trucks over the next decade.
  • Updated Carbon Intensity Tables Released for Draft Discussion Rules of the New Mexico Clean Transportation Fuel Program The New Mexico Environment Department (NMED) is actively engaged in the development of the Clean Transportation Fuel Program (CTFP). The NMED has released updated carbon intensity tables for the draft discussion rules posted publicly on December 20, 2024. The information is preliminary and may be modified as research, modelling, public engagement, and input from Tribes, Pueblos, and Nations and interested parties progresses.
